q: Summarize the book of Genesis: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Moses; c. 1446–1406 B.C. (the wilderness period).

**Genre.** Narrative / Law (Torah).

**Theme.** Beginnings: God creates a good world, humanity falls into sin, and God answers that ruin by launching redemption through a covenant with Abraham's family.

**Outline.**
- 1–11 — Primeval history: creation, fall, flood, Babel
- 12–25 — Abraham: the call and covenant promises
- 25–28 — Isaac and the passing of the promise
- 28–36 — Jacob and the twelve tribes
- 37–50 — Joseph and the descent into Egypt

**Christ & significance.** The first promise of the gospel (3:15) and the Abrahamic covenant (12:1–3) chart the trajectory of redemptive history that is fulfilled in Christ, the promised seed in whom all nations are blessed.
summary: Moses, c. 1446–1406 B.C. Beginnings: creation, fall, flood, and Babel (1–11), then the patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and Joseph (12–50). The protoevangelium (3:15) and Abrahamic covenant launch redemptive history.

q: Summarize the book of Exodus: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Moses; c. 1446–1406 B.C., recounting the deliverance from Egypt.

**Genre.** Narrative / Law (Torah).

**Theme.** Redemption and covenant: the LORD remembers His promise to Abraham, rescues Israel from slavery by the Passover and the sea, binds them to Himself at Sinai, and comes to dwell among them.

**Outline.**
- 1–6 — Bondage in Egypt and the call of Moses
- 7–12 — The plagues and the Passover
- 13–18 — The exodus and journey to Sinai
- 19–24 — The covenant and the giving of the Law
- 25–40 — The tabernacle and God's glory dwelling with Israel

**Christ & significance.** The Passover lamb and the exodus deliverance prefigure Christ, our Passover (1 Cor 5:7), who redeems His people from bondage and dwells with them; the tabernacle anticipates God tabernacling among us in the incarnate Word.
summary: Moses, c. 1446–1406 B.C. God redeems Israel from Egyptian slavery through the Passover and the sea (1–18), gives the Law at Sinai (19–24), and dwells with His people in the tabernacle (25–40). The Passover foreshadows Christ our Passover.

q: Summarize the book of Leviticus: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Moses; c. 1446–1406 B.C., given at Sinai after the tabernacle was raised.

**Genre.** Law (Torah).

**Theme.** Holiness: how a sinful people may approach and live with a holy God, through atoning sacrifice, a mediating priesthood, and consecrated life.

**Outline.**
- 1–7 — The sacrificial system and offerings
- 8–10 — The ordination and ministry of the priests
- 11–15 — Laws of clean and unclean
- 16 — The Day of Atonement
- 17–27 — The Holiness Code and covenant blessings

**Christ & significance.** The sacrifices, priesthood, and Day of Atonement foreshadow Christ, the final sacrifice and great High Priest (Heb 9–10), whose blood cleanses and who makes His people holy: "Be holy, for I am holy."
summary: Moses, c. 1446–1406 B.C. The manual of holiness: sacrifices and offerings (1–7), priesthood (8–10), clean/unclean (11–15), the Day of Atonement (16), and the Holiness Code (17–27). Its sacrifices and high priest point to Christ.

q: Summarize the book of Numbers: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Moses; c. 1446–1406 B.C., spanning the wilderness wanderings.

**Genre.** Narrative / Law (Torah).

**Theme.** God's faithfulness amid Israel's unbelief: an unfaithful generation forfeits the land through grumbling and rebellion, yet the LORD preserves a covenant people and brings a new generation to the border of Canaan.

**Outline.**
- 1–10 — Census and preparation at Sinai
- 11–14 — Rebellion and the refusal to enter Canaan
- 15–19 — Wilderness laws and further rebellions
- 20–25 — The journey of the new generation
- 26–36 — Second census and readiness for the land

**Christ & significance.** The bronze serpent lifted up (21:8–9) is named by Jesus as a sign of His own saving death (John 3:14–15); God's patient preservation of a wandering people displays the persevering grace fulfilled in Christ.
summary: Moses, c. 1446–1406 B.C. Israel's wilderness journey: census and preparation (1–10), rebellion and forty years of wandering (11–25), and a new generation readied for Canaan (26–36). The lifted-up serpent prefigures Christ crucified.

q: Summarize the book of Deuteronomy: author, date, theme, and outline.
a:
**Author & date.** Moses; c. 1406 B.C., his farewell addresses on the plains of Moab.

**Genre.** Law / Covenant renewal (Torah).

**Theme.** Covenant renewal: on the eve of entering Canaan, Moses calls the new generation to wholehearted love and obedience to the LORD, setting before them the blessing of life and the curse of death.

**Outline.**
- 1–4 — First address: review of the wilderness journey
- 5–11 — Second address: the great commandment to love God
- 12–26 — Covenant stipulations and laws
- 27–30 — Blessings, curses, and the call to choose life
- 31–34 — Moses' final words, song, and death

**Christ & significance.** Deuteronomy promises a prophet like Moses (18:15), fulfilled in Christ, who quotes this book to defeat temptation and embodies the wholehearted love of God; its blessings and curses anticipate the gospel where Christ bears the curse for us.
summary: Moses, c. 1406 B.C. Moses' farewell sermons on Moab's plains renew the covenant with a new generation, calling them to love and obey God amid blessing and curse. It promises a prophet like Moses, fulfilled in Christ.
